hook: You already know that a line can intersect a circle in two points, one point, or no points. You've also worked with angles formed by two chords inside a circle. But what happens when the lines are outside the circle — when one line just grazes the circle and another cuts all the way through it? The angle formed and the arc lengths involved follow a specific rule that you can't yet calculate. Can you figure out the relationship between the angle formed outside a circle and the two arcs it intercepts?
concept: A tangent line touches a circle at exactly one point (called the point of tangency), while a secant line intersects a circle at exactly two points. When two such lines meet at a point outside the circle, the angle they form equals half the (positive) difference of the intercepted arcs: $m\angle = \frac{1}{2}(\text{far arc} - \text{near arc})$. This same formula applies whether two secants, two tangents, or one of each meet outside the circle.
anatomy: Every tangent-secant configuration outside a circle has five key parts. The external point is where the two lines meet outside the circle. The far arc (sometimes called the major intercepted arc) is the larger arc cut off between the two intersection points with the circle — it is the arc farther from the external point. The near arc (minor intercepted arc) is the smaller arc between those same two points — it sits closest to the external point. The intercepted arc difference is $\text{far arc} - \text{near arc}$, always taken as positive. Finally, the exterior angle is the angle at the external point, equal to $\frac{1}{2}(\text{far arc} - \text{near arc})$.

worked_example.expression_latex: \text{Two secants from external point } P. \text{ Far arc} = 150^\circ, \text{ Near arc} = 60^\circ. \text{ Find } m\angle P.
worked_example.description_latex: We begin by identifying which arc is 'far' and which is 'near.' The far arc (150°) is the arc that lies between the two secants on the side away from point P, and the near arc (60°) is the arc between the secants closest to P. We will apply the exterior angle formula.

This is the core formula for any angle formed outside a circle by two secants, two tangents, or a tangent and a secant. We always subtract the smaller (near) arc from the larger (far) arc before halving, because taking half the difference gives the exterior angle.
Substituting our arc measures, we subtract 60° from 150° to get 90°. This difference represents how much larger the far arc is compared to the near arc, and halving it gives us the exterior angle.

Dividing 90° by 2 gives us 45°. Notice that the exterior angle (45°) is much smaller than either arc — this makes sense because the angle is formed far from the circle, so it appears compressed compared to the arcs it intercepts.

partial_example.expression_latex: \text{A tangent and a secant from external point } Q. \text{ Far arc} = 200^\circ, \text{ Near arc} = ?. \text{ Whole circle} = 360^\circ. \text{ Find } m\angle Q = 65^\circ.
partial_example.description_latex: We are working backwards this time — we know the exterior angle is 65° and the far arc is 200°, and we need to find the near arc. Start by writing out the exterior angle formula and substituting what you know.

Here we have let $x$ represent the unknown near arc and plugged into the formula. The next key step is to isolate the expression $(200^\circ - x)$ by multiplying both sides by 2 — try completing that algebra step.
Multiply both sides by 2 to clear the fraction, then solve for $x$ (the near arc). Write each line of algebra clearly and state the value of the near arc.

Multiplying both sides by 2 gives $130^\circ = 200^\circ - x$, so $x = 70^\circ$. You can verify this makes sense: $\frac{1}{2}(200^\circ - 70^\circ) = \frac{1}{2}(130^\circ) = 65^\circ$ ✓, and both arcs add to 270°, which is less than 360° — so there is a third arc not part of this configuration.

practice_problems.prompt_latex: \text{Two secants meet at an external point. The far arc is } 120^\circ \text{ and the near arc is } 40^\circ. \text{ Find the exterior angle.}
\text{A tangent and a secant meet at an external point. The far arc is } 180^\circ \text{ and the near arc is } 80^\circ. \text{ Find the exterior angle.}
\text{Two tangents from an external point intercept a major arc of } 240^\circ. \text{ The minor arc is } 360^\circ - 240^\circ = 120^\circ. \text{ Find the exterior angle.}

\text{Two secants from point } P \text{ form a } 35^\circ \text{ exterior angle. The far arc is } (3x+10)^\circ \text{ and the near arc is } (x-10)^\circ. \text{ Find } x \text{ and both arc measures.}

\text{A security camera at point } C \text{ outside a circular fountain can see along two tangent lines to the fountain. The near arc (hidden side) spans } 100^\circ. \text{ What is the measure of the far arc, and what angle does the camera's field of view form?}

common_mistakes: Students add the two arcs instead of subtracting them, computing $\frac{1}{2}(\text{far arc} + \text{near arc})$ — this is the formula for an inscribed angle or a chord-chord angle inside the circle, not an exterior angle. Always subtract when the vertex is outside the circle.

Students forget to halve the difference and set the exterior angle equal to $(\text{far arc} - \text{near arc})$ directly, yielding an answer twice too large. Remember the factor of $\frac{1}{2}$ is required in every exterior angle case.

When two tangents are drawn from an external point, students mistakenly use only the minor arc (the near arc) in the formula and ignore the major arc entirely. Both arcs must be identified — the major arc is the far arc that wraps around the back of the circle, and both are needed for the subtraction.
untested_variants: Finding a missing arc when the two arcs are expressed as algebraic expressions (e.g., $(5x-10)^\circ$ and $(2x+20)^\circ$) and the exterior angle is also given as an expression, requiring students to set up and solve a multi-step equation.
Problems where three or more secants or tangents are drawn from the same external point, requiring students to identify which two lines and which pair of arcs form the angle being asked about.

Proofs or justification problems asking students to derive the exterior angle formula using the triangle formed by the external point and the two intersection points on the circle, connecting it to the exterior angle theorem for triangles.
Coordinate geometry problems where the circle has a given equation and students must find the equations of tangent or secant lines from an external point before applying the arc-angle relationships.
